children.text: Eightieth session – Agenda item 155 – Financing of the United Nations Multidimensional Integrated Stabilization Mission in Mali – Budget performance of the United Nations Multidimensional Integrated Stabilization Mission in Mali for the period from 1 July 2024 to 30 June 2025 – Report of the Secretary-General
Contents

Summary of follow-up action taken to implement the decisions and requests of the General Assembly in its resolution 78/250 B, including the requests and recommendations of the Advisory Committee on Administrative and Budgetary Questions endorsed by the Assembly

Summary of follow-up action taken to implement the decisions and requests of the General Assembly in its resolution 78/250 B, including the requests and recommendations of the Advisory Committee on Administrative and Budgetary Questions endorsed by the Assembly

Summary of follow-up action taken to implement the decisions and requests of the General Assembly in its resolution 78/250 B, including the requests and recommendations of the Advisory Committee on Administrative and Budgetary Questions endorsed by the Assembly
Advisory Committee on Administrative and Budgetary Questions
Advisory Committee on Administrative and Budgetary Questions
Advisory Committee on Administrative and Budgetary Questions

Total expenditure for the United Nations Multidimensional Integrated Stabilization Mission in Mali (MINUSMA) for the period from 1 July 2024 to 30 June 2025 has been linked to the Mission’s objectives through a results-based budgeting framework.

Following the adoption of Security Council resolution 2690 (2023), MINUSMA focused on a timely and orderly drawdown, withdrawal and liquidation. From 1 July 2024 to 30 June 2025, MINUSMA focused on asset disposition and the planned repatriation of the remaining staff, contingent-owned equipment and rear parties. While the initial plan was to complete in situ liquidation by 31 March 2025, owing to the deterioration in the security situation in Mali and following the request from the transitional Government of Mali to complete the closure of MINUSMA by 31 December 2024, the Mission accelerated the liquidation and handed over Bamako headquarters to the host Government on 15 November 2024.

MINUSMA incurred expenditure totalling $164.8 million for the reporting period, representing a resource utilization rate of 81.3 per cent, compared with $833.8 million in expenditure and a resource utilization rate of 96.2 per cent in the 2023/24 period.

The unencumbered balance of $38.0 million reflects the net impact of: (a) reduced requirements under civilian personnel ($17.6 million), attributable primarily to the accelerated closures at Gao and Bamako; and (b) reduced requirements under operational costs ($31.5 million), attributable primarily to lower freight costs due to the decision to dispose of more United Nations property in situ rather than transfer them out of Mali on the basis of a cost-benefit analysis.
The reduced requirements were offset in part by increased requirements for military and police personnel ($11.1 million) due to provisions to settle potential claims for pending liabilities.
(Thousands of United States dollars; budget year is from 1 July 2024 to 30 June 2025)
a	Represents the highest level of authorized/proposed deployment for the period from 1 January to 30 June 2024 and proposed deployment for the period from 1 July 2024 to 30 June 2025.
The actions to be taken by the General Assembly are set out in section VI of the present report.

children.text: 1.	The proposed budget for the liquidation of the United Nations Multidimensional Integrated Stabilization Mission in Mali (MINUSMA) for the period from 1 July 2024 to 30 June 2025 was set out in the report of the Secretary-General dated 7 February 2024 (A/78/761) and amounted to $207,080,300 (gross) and $199,317,500 (net). It provided for the deployment of 190 military contingent personnel, 7 United Nations police officers, 140 formed police unit personnel, 229 international staff, 179 national staff and 14 United Nations Volunteers, as well as a provision for general temporary assistance positions for surge capacity.

2.	In its report dated 8 April 2024, the Advisory Committee on Administrative and Budgetary Questions recommended that the General Assembly appropriate $207,080,300 (gross) for the period from 1 July 2024 to 30 June 2025 (A/78/744/Add.11).
3.	The General Assembly, by its resolution 78/250 B, appropriated an amount of $202,783,500 for the maintenance of the Mission for the period from 1 July 2024 to 30 June 2025.
A.	Overall

4.	The mandate of MINUSMA was established by the Security Council in its resolution 2100 (2013) and extended in subsequent resolutions of the Council. The mandate for the performance reporting period was provided by the Council in its resolution 2690 (2023).
5.	The Security Council, in paragraph 1 of resolution 2690 (2023), decided to terminate the mandate of MINUSMA under resolution 2640 (2022) as from 30 June 2023.

6.	The Security Council decided that the liquidation of MINUSMA should begin on 1 January 2024 and authorized, for the duration of the Mission’s liquidation, the retention of a United Nations Guard Unit from within the Mission’s existing footprint to protect MINUSMA personnel, facilities and assets.
7.	The Security Council requested MINUSMA to ensure that its team sites and assets were handed over in in full compliance with general United Nations practices and financial regulations.
B.	Budget implementation
Liquidation activities during the period from 1 July 2024 to 31 December 2024

8.	As at 1 July 2024, MINUSMA maintained only two locations: its headquarters in Bamako and the camp in Gao. All other sites had been closed and handed over to the host Government, with the exception of three camps in Sector North (Tessalit, Aguelhok and Kidal), from which the Mission had to withdraw owing to the deteriorating security situation. That streamlined presence in Mali allowed the Mission to remain prepared for any contingency, including the potential need for an accelerated closure of one or both of the remaining locations.

9.	In response to the overall deterioration in the security situation and to ensure the safety of personnel and assets, MINUSMA, using the capabilities of the Guard Unit and the security service contractor, undertook measures to strengthen security at the Bamako headquarters. The measures included enhanced surveillance, regular patrols, reinforced access control and tighter monitoring of personnel and vehicles entering and exiting the premises.

10.	The Mission maintained its overall structure, with the Assistant Secretary-General and Head of Entity leading liaison efforts with the transitional Government of Mali, the United Nations country team and regional organizations. The Head of Entity also continued to perform the functions of Resident Coordinator and Humanitarian Coordinator. Following the departure of the Head of Entity on 19 August 2024, the Director of the liquidation team (D-2) assumed the responsibilities of the Head of Entity.

11.	At the start of the performance period, MINUSMA retained its Guard Unit, consisting of 190 military personnel deployed to Gao, along with 3 individual police officers, to ensure the safety and security of staff and assets at that location. In addition, 140 formed police unit personnel, accompanied by 4 individual police officers, were stationed at the Bamako headquarters.

12.	As at 1 July 2024, MINUSMA had 52 standard 20-foot containers of contingent-owned equipment ready for repatriation. The total asset holdings of the entity as at 1 July 2024 was valued at $129.6 million, including capitalized equipment with a net book value of $29.3 million, equipment below capitalization threshold with a residual value of $18.7 million, consumables and supplies valued at $68.7 million and non-serialized equipment valued at $12.9 million.

13.	The original liquidation plan included the closure of all locations in Mali by 31 March 2025, with the camp in Gao to be handed over by 30 September 2024 and the headquarters in Bamako by 31 March 2025. However, owing to the deteriorating security situation and the increasing complexity of the operational environment, contingency plans were developed at the same time to enable the entity to accelerate its liquidation activities, including the earlier closure of locations and the complete repatriation of personnel.

14.	Such an approach proved effective when MINUSMA received a note verbale from the transitional Government of Mali on 16 February 2024 in response to the submitted liquidation plan. The note included a request to accelerate the closure and ensure the complete departure of MINUSMA by 31 December 2024.

15.	In the light of the new timeline provided by the host Government, MINUSMA activated its contingency measures and intensified coordination with United Nations Headquarters, the United Nations Logistics Base at Brindisi, Italy, and the Regional Service Centre in Entebbe, Uganda, to ensure a timely closure.

16.	The disengagement plan was developed specifically to address the accelerated closure of locations during the 2024/25 period. It outlined the phased discontinuation of services at MINUSMA headquarters, following the closure of the Gao camp, which was brought forward to 22 July 2024, and preparations for the handover of the Bamako headquarters, rescheduled for 15 November 2024. The plan also set out timelines and action for the residual team remaining in Mali after the handover of the Bamako headquarters, with activities to be completed by the end of December 2024. In addition, staffing capacities were allocated for tasks to be carried out at non-field locations in Brindisi and Entebbe, covering asset disposal, environment, finance, budget and human resources functions.

17.	The United Nations Logistics Base supported MINUSMA in finalizing the implementation of the asset disposal plan, providing on-site assistance in property management and warehouse operations, including training staff of the entity on reporting and disposal procedures. The Logistics Base played a crucial role in the purchase order diversion process, identified receiving missions for mission assets available to be diverted and facilitated the inter-mission transfer of those assets. In addition, the Logistics Base facilitated the shipment of designated assets for inclusion in the return, refurbishment and reuse (3R) programme.

18.	The United Nations Logistics Base also continued to support the entity in environmental management, providing guidance on the environmental close-out assessment based on lessons learned from previous liquidation processes, options for the safe and responsible disposal of hazardous waste and advice on the on-site disposal of other wastes.

19.	The Regional Service Centre provided administrative services in human resources, finance and travel. This included the check‐out and separation processes, the processing of final payments, the handling of pension documentation, the processing of entitlement payments, reviews of time and attendance records, management of payroll, review of and follow up on open account balances and finalization of the books of accounts.

20.	United Nations Headquarters facilitated the movement of contingent-owned and United Nations-owned equipment through long-term charter contracts, provided overall guidance in the liquidation process and ensured close coordination with representatives of troop- and police-contributing countries.
Closure of Gao camp

21.	Under the responsibility of the environmental and waste management focal point of the contingents, troop- and police-contributing countries were involved in the initial cleanup, given that the preparations for repatriation were expected to generate a significant volume of general waste. The Mission took over the final cleanup of the camps after the departure of rear parties and prior to the camp’s handover.

22.	By 1 July 2024, the entity had completed the environmental close-out assessment for the Gao camp and finalized cleanup activities, including general waste disposal, bioremediation of contaminated soil and septic tank cleanup. The disposal of hazardous waste was managed by a dedicated contractor. A joint inspection with Malian authorities was conducted from 2 to 4 July 2024, and all observations from the host Government team were addressed prior to the site handover. In line with the standard operating procedures for the MINUSMA closure, the Mine Action Service completed an explosives risk assessment and survey of the Gao camp on 18 July 2024.

23.	The camp was officially handed over to Malian authorities on 22 July 2024. The handover included the camp’s infrastructure, facilities and essential equipment, as well as assets located at the local airport. Efforts were made to ensure that all facilities and equipment were in proper working order, and staff of the transitional Government of Mali were familiarized with the operation and maintenance of the facilities and equipment.

24.	The disposal of United Nations property in Gao was carried out in compliance with the Financial Regulations and Rules of the United Nations. The remaining United Nations-owned equipment was transferred to holding areas outside Mali, including Dakar and Lomé.

25.	The closure of the camp in Gao marked the end of MINUSMA air operations and initiated the handover of airport and movement control facilities in Bamako. The transfer of air region 1 at the airport from the entity to Mali’s airports authority took place on 15 August 2024. A bilaterally signed certificate confirmed that MINUSMA had vacated the site and that the airport authority had inspected and accepted the site and its assets. The handover included a detailed inventory of United Nations-owned equipment and materials remaining on site. An environmental assessment was also conducted to ensure compliance with United Nations and local standards, with all necessary cleanup and mitigation measures completed prior to the handover.
Closure of Bamako headquarters

26.	By July 2024, all milestones and liquidation objectives for the headquarters in Bamako had been identified and the handover date set for 15 November 2024, at the request of the transitional Government of Mali, to accelerate the closure. Plans were developed for the commercial sale of United Nations-owned equipment, inter-mission transfers were initiated and wall-to-wall surveys were completed. Timelines for contract terminations were formulated as part of the disengagement plan, along with schedules for discontinuing services.

27.	MINUSMA undertook extensive efforts to ensure that the Bamako headquarters remained fully functional up to the handover date, including maintaining facilities in good condition and ensuring that all infrastructure and equipment were in proper working order. Preparations for the handover included environmental remediation activities, repairs and maintenance, infrastructure upgrades, security enhancements and other necessary improvements.

28.	Cleanup efforts in Bamako included sorting construction waste, collecting 2,050 m3 of general waste and 12.5 m3 of incinerated non-hazardous waste. Environmental remediation activities focused on cleaning up both the Bamako headquarters and the bulk fuel installation at Bamako International Airport. A continuity plan was developed with local health providers to maintain essential medical services throughout the liquidation period.

29.	MINUSMA advanced its disengagement by closing the transport workshop in October 2024 and outsourcing vehicle maintenance to support the remaining fleet until December, while progressively scaling down transport operations and reallocating or selling vehicles. It completed the depletion of ration and fuel reserves, terminated related contracts and decommissioned all fuel and rations facilities by mid-November. Communications and information technology infrastructure was progressively shut down, and all frequencies were returned to the transitional Government of Mali.

30.	The disposition of United Nations property in Bamako was conducted in accordance with United Nations regulations and rules and the asset disposition plan. Assets and equipment in good condition were redeployed to other peacekeeping operations and United Nations entities. High-value assets identified for inclusion in the 3R programme were redeployed to the United Nations Logistics Base.

31.	While the Mine Action Service concluded its on-site presence in Mali in August 2024, in support of the environmental assessment and the handover of the Bamako headquarters to Malian authorities, two Mine Action Service technical specialists were deployed to Bamako in November 2024 to complete the risk assessment ahead of the formal camp handover.

32.	The liquidation team successfully handed over MINUSMA headquarters in Bamako to the representatives of the transitional Government of Mali on 15 November 2024. The camp was transferred in fully functional condition, with all life support systems operational, including water treatment, sewage and electrical systems.

33.	To finalize in situ residual liquidation activities, the liquidation team, in coordination with the office of the resident coordinator in Mali and the United Nations Development Programme country office, arranged for office space to accommodate a limited number of staff and individual contractors at the United Nations common house in Mali through 15 December 2024.

34.	The team’s primary focus was on settling outstanding obligations and terminating contracts with local counterparts, finalizing movement operations within Mali, completing checkout formalities and overseeing the subsequent reassignment or separation of staff, disposal of assets and bank reconciliation.
35.	By 15 December 2024, all national staff had been separated from the liquidation team and all international staff had departed, marking the conclusion of the physical presence of MINUSMA in Mali.
Non-field based residual liquidation activities from 1 January to 30 June 2025

36.	In January 2025, the liquidation residual teams (deployed to the Regional Service Centre and to the United Nations Logistics Base) became fully operational in line with the accelerated timelines of the MINUSMA liquidation plan.

37.	During the deployment to the Regional Service Centre, nine staff members performed human resources, finance and budget functions to address all residual issues arising from the closure of operations in Bamako. The finance and budget stream managed 1,614 open items and closely monitored the status of the entity’s accounts receivable and accounts payable. Payments for repatriated staff with separation packages were processed without delay. In collaboration with United Nations Headquarters, the liquidation team continued the closure of MINUSMA accounts. The human resources team monitored and ensured the quality of all MINUSMA staff records, in collaboration with the Regional Service Centre.

38.	Given that the Regional Service Centre was expected to assume responsibility for the finance and budget functions after the repatriation of the team, the liquidation team established coordination mechanisms with the Regional Service Centre to ensure a seamless handover of outstanding issues.

39.	With regard to property management, 17 staff members were deployed to the United Nations Logistics base and processed and reconciled the remaining asset disposal transactions. The work was carried out in close collaboration with the Logistics Base and with support from United Nations Headquarters, enabling the expeditious completion of non-field-based transactional activities. In addition, they established protocols for the continuous enhancement of data quality, ensuring the integrity of system records and the accurate presentation of the disposition of MINUSMA property. As at 1 January 2025, MINUSMA had remaining holdings with a residual value of $54.1 million, all of which were fully processed by 30 June 2025.
C.	Mission support initiatives
Lessons learned

40.	The liquidation team compiled lessons learned on how policies, guidelines and procedures either contributed to or hindered the withdrawal and liquidation processes, and developed related recommendations. The most relevant ones are presented below.
(a)	Movement of United Nations-owned equipment and contingent-owned equipment outside Mali

41.	As MINUSMA progressed through its liquidation phase, several logistical and operational challenges highlighted the need for more centralized and standardized processes. One of the primary lessons learned was the importance of centralizing freight-forwarding services through global freight contracts, coordinated by United Nations Headquarters. As a mission draws down and loses internal capacity, a centralized contracting mechanism would allow for more consistent, efficient and cost-effective management of shipments, in particular inter-mission transfers of United Nations-owned equipment. In addition, statements of work for freight services must be enhanced to include minimum vehicle mobility requirements suited to terrain conditions and to clearly define subcontractor responsibilities and limits in order to ensure compliance with insurance, labour and safety standards.

42.	Cross-border logistics were another significant concern. The lack of memorandums of understanding with neighbouring countries for road movements at mission start-up led to planning inaccuracies and delays during withdrawal. Future missions should establish such agreements concurrently with the status of forces agreement at the mission’s inception, where possible.

43.	In addition, late identification of unserviceable ammunition posed serious repatriation challenges. There is a critical need for the early identification, proper packaging and documentation of ammunition, in compliance with international safety standards.
(b)	Human resources
44.	During the liquidation of MINUSMA, several human resources and administrative challenges emerged, offering valuable lessons for future mission closures.

45.	Engagement with the Regional Service Centre and the United Nations Logistics Base at least three months before separation of personnel is crucial for an early and coordinated separation planning, and it can help to manage transaction volumes and facilitate repatriation planning. The timely and accurate completion of separation documents, ideally two months before the separation date, is also critical. Human resources clinics and one-stop checkout locations are recommended to improve process efficiency and ensure that staff receive appropriate guidance. Accurate and regularly updated staff data are essential for effective separation planning and comparative reviews. Missions must ensure early engagement with support centres (the Logistics Base and the Regional Service Centre).

46.	In terms of broader staff support, initiatives such as job fairs, transition coaching and capacity-building programmes, in particular for national staff, were deemed essential. These efforts should be complemented by proactive outreach to other United Nations entities to facilitate the placement of staff with priority consideration status. To address the psychological and emotional toll during mission closures, staff support measures, including stress management guidance, access to staff counsellors and resilience training, proved to be highly effective.
(c)	Streamlining the disposal of assets process

47.	During the liquidation of MINUSMA, several obstacles related to asset transfer and property management emerged, underscoring the need for system improvements. One key issue was the complexity of the property derecognition process, in particular in transferring assets to United Nations agencies, funds and programmes within the United Nations country team. Simplified transfers at residual value, with the appropriate safeguards to prevent loss to the Organization, are recommended in order to enable smoother collaboration with United Nations agencies, funds and programmes.

48.	Lastly, the importance of regular physical verification and real-time tracking of assets is needed to ensure data accuracy and accountability, and the use of advanced technologies for improved asset management is recommended.
(d)	Programmatic activities and implementing partners
49.	As part of the liquidation process, peacekeeping missions are expected to complete the financial closure of programmatic activities within the validity period of their agreements.

50.	To support timely closure, the Umoja grantor module should be the central platform for documenting all project-related transactions, including agreements, monitoring, reports and decisions. Missions must ensure that all key documents are uploaded to this system in order to promote transparency and accountability.

51.	During liquidation, missions must retain sufficient capacity to manage the financial closure of programmatic activities, even after substantive teams depart. This capability should be integrated into the finance team.

52.	MINUSMA faced challenges owing to the lack of capacities of some implementing partners, which delayed project transfers. Missions should improve the vetting and due diligence processes during the selection of implementing partners and promote the sharing of underperformance cases with other United Nations entities. Clear handover guidelines for transferring projects to other United Nations entities should also be developed.
(e)	Environmental mitigation and rehabilitation practices in closing peacekeeping missions

53.	MINUSMA established the footprint and environment working group with clear terms of reference for the Mission’s drawdown. This forum was used to develop how to gradually minimize the MINUSMA footprint and to deal with the environmental aspects required for the closure in line with the Mission’s integrated drawdown plan.

54.	The involvement of uniformed personnel in the initial cleanup of contingent environmental and waste proved effective in managing substantial general waste produced during repatriation preparations. The mission support team completed the final cleanup of camps after the departure of rear parties or before camp handover.
(f)	Surge capacity

55.	The liquidation of MINUSMA has revealed several key lessons related to staffing and operational support. One recurring issue has been staffing gaps caused by the departure of liquidation team members. To address this, it is essential that up-to-date operational rosters be maintained of staff with specialized expertise who can be rapidly deployed during the liquidation period, when staff turnover is common and continuity is critical. MINUSMA had to rely heavily on standing surge capacity and temporary duty assignments to fill urgent skill gaps. In some cases, in particular in functions such as procurement and movement control, specific language skills were necessary to engage effectively with local contractors and stakeholders.

56.	In the light of these lessons, several recommendations have been proposed. First, the standing surge capacity should be expanded to include additional technical roles tailored to liquidation needs. The standard operating procedure for deploying this capacity should be revised to guarantee deployment within two to three weeks and to include mechanisms for resolving competing demands among releasing entities. Second, the surge pool should be diversified to include staff with various language skills, and roster membership could be reconsidered to increase flexibility. Building a pool of retirees or former staff to supplement surge deployments could be also explored.
D.	Results-based budgeting frameworks
Component 1: support

57.	The liquidation entails providing rapid, effective, efficient and responsible services to support the liquidation through the delivery of related outputs. The range of support encompassed the implementation of conduct and discipline, personnel administration, financial management services, healthcare, maintenance of office and accommodation facilities, information and communications technology, air and surface transport operations, supply and resupply operations and provision of security services.

Maintenance of a contractual arrangement with a stand-alone commercial aero-medical evacuation team in two locations, Bamako and Gao, to support aero-medical evacuations within and outside Mali to advanced medical facilities for critical life- and limb-saving stabilization procedures

Maintenance of a contractual arrangement with a stand-alone commercial aero-medical evacuation team in two locations, Bamako and Gao, to support aero-medical evacuations within and outside Mali to advanced medical facilities for critical life- and limb-saving stabilization procedures
Provision of patient evacuation coordination cell services to MINUSMA personnel in Bamako supporting two liquidation locations, Bamako and Gao
Provision of staff counselling services to MINUSMA personnel
100 per cent physical verification of all property, plant and equipment and inventory during the liquidation period
Timely initiation of write-off and disposal processes prior to the physical closure and handover of liquidation, camps in full compliance with the Financial Regulations and Rules
Write off, disposal and reporting of property, plant and equipment, and inventory
Vehicle management and ground transportation services
Operation, maintenance and gradual disposal of up to 196 United Nations-owned vehicles assigned to the liquidation and closure team through two workshops
Operation, maintenance and gradual disposal of up to 196 United Nations-owned vehicles assigned to the liquidation and closure team through two workshops
Provision of daily shuttle services, 7 days a week, transporting the liquidation personnel within the area designated for the liquidation operations
Provision of security services, 24 hours a day, 7 days a week, through the two locations during the liquidation period
Conduct of up to 900 prevention patrols per month
Provision of 24-hour close protection services to senior personnel and visiting high-level officials
Conduct of fire safety prevention evaluation and assessments, conducting 90 verification patrols per month
Provision of fire safety awareness and 2 fire safety training sessions for the remaining personnel per month
Coordination of monthly crisis/incident management coordination exercises with the Guard Unit
Conduct of security awareness and emergency drills for the personnel arriving to MINUSMA on surge capacity
12 internal reports on Mali, including media reports, misinformation and disinformation
Media and social media internal reports
1 iSeek multimedia report on the closure of the liquidation process
6 articles on the activities of the United Nations in Mali post-Mission, to be published on the United Nations Mali web platforms
3 media events on the post-MINUSMA activities of the United Nations in Mali
1 campaign on the United Nations work in Mali across key Malian local radios, in local languages
United Nations human rights due diligence policy
Conduct 30 risk assessments in line with the human rights due diligence policy in the context of camp closures and transfers, gifting and sales of United Nations-owned equipment
Disposal of ammunition and explosives and completion of the survey and certification of explosive ordnance risk assessments of camps prior to being handed over to the Transition Government

The disposal of ammunition in the country was not possible owing to the absence of approval from the host Government. A total of 103 tons of ammunition was shipped outside of Mali for destruction. The Mine Action Service surveyed and issued certifications of explosive ordnance risk assessments for the United Nations camp in Gao and Bamako
Implementation of a conduct and discipline programme for all police/military and civilian personnel through prevention, including training; and monitoring of investigations and disciplinary action

Note: The amounts represent the total obligations that were cancelled at the end of the 12-month period following the end of the budget period in respect of goods supplied and services rendered in the budget period, and at the end of an additional period of four years in respect of obligations to troop- and police-contributing countries.
D.	Other revenue and adjustments, and borrowing/lending
1.	Other revenue and adjustments
(Thousands of United States dollars)
a 	Breakdown of other/miscellaneous revenue (thousands of United States dollars): refund of prior period expenses ($98.8); and other revenue ($12,928.0).
2.	Borrowing
59.	MINUSMA did not issue loans to or borrow from other active peacekeeping missions during the reporting period.

60.	The increased requirements were attributable primarily to: (a) provisions for the reimbursements to troop-contributing countries due to forced abandonment and the destruction of ammunition and explosives; and (b) provisions to settle claims for pending liabilities from commercial vendors for rations.

61.	The increased requirements were offset in part by lower amounts of standard troop reimbursements and of contingent-owned equipment due to the earlier-than-planned withdrawal of contingents following the closure of the camp in Gao.

62.	The decreased requirements were attributable primarily to lower reimbursement amounts of formed police unit costs, rations and contingent-owned equipment self-sustainment due to the earlier-than-planned withdrawal of formed police units following the closure of the Mission’s headquarters in Bamako.

63.	The decreased requirements were attributable primarily to lower amounts of salaries and common staff costs of international staff due to their earlier-than-planned separation following the closures at Gao and Bamako.

64.	The decreased requirements were attributable primarily to lower amounts of salaries of National Professional Officers due to their earlier-than-planned separation following the closures at Gao and Bamako.

65.	The decreased requirements were offset in part by the payment of termination indemnities to National Professional Officers due to their earlier-than-planned separation following the closures at Gao and Bamako.

66.	The increased requirements were attributable primarily to the payment of termination indemnities to national General Service staff due to their earlier-than-planned separation following the closures at Gao and Bamako.
67.	The increased requirements were offset in part by lower amounts of salaries of national General Service staff due to their earlier-than-planned departure following the closures at Gao and Bamako.
68.	The decreased requirements were attributable primarily to lower payments to United Nations Volunteers due to their earlier-than-planned departure following the closures at Gao and Bamako.

69.	The increased requirements were attributable primarily to higher number of months needed for surge capacity for filling critical functions during the liquidation phases of the Mission due to the high turnover of international staff.
70.	The decreased requirements were attributable primarily to the greater use of internal capacity.

71.	The decreased requirements were attributable primarily to the prioritization of temporary appointments of general temporary assistance positions, which led to a lower number of trips of staff on temporary duty assignment.

72.	The decreased requirements were attributable primarily to reduced security and maintenance services of premises and the reduced acquisition of spare parts and supplies due to the earlier-than-planned closures at Gao and Bamako.
73.	The decreased requirements were offset in part by provisions to settle potential claims from fuel contractors for demurrage, customs, penalties and other charges related to the Timbuktu closure.
74.	The increased requirements were attributable primarily to provisions to settle potential claims for previous periods for rotary-wing aircraft rentals, pending confirmation of services rendered.
75.	The increased requirements were offset in part by reduced requirements for fixed-wing aircraft operations, given that the entity ceased air operations after the closure of the camp in Gao.
76.	The decreased requirements were attributable primarily to the accelerated drawdown of information technology infrastructure due to the earlier-than-planned closures at Gao and Bamako.
77.	The decreased requirements were attributable primarily to the earlier-than-planned closure of the remaining clinics in Gao and Bamako.

78.	The decreased requirements were attributable primarily to lower freight costs due to the decision to dispose of more United Nations property in situ rather than transfer them outside Mali on the basis of the cost-benefit analysis.

text: VI.	Actions to be taken by the General Assembly

children.text: 79.	The actions to be taken by the General Assembly in connection with the financing of the United Nations Multidimensional Integrated Stabilization Mission in Mali are:
(a)	To take note of the unencumbered balance of $37,977,600 with respect to the period from 1 July 2024 to 30 June 2025;

(b)	To take note of other revenue for the period ended 30 June 2025 amounting to $73,206,100 from investment revenue ($8,227,500), other/ miscellaneous revenue ($13,026,800) and the cancellation of prior-period obligations ($51,951,800);

(c)	To defer, until consideration by the General Assembly of the final performance report of the Mission, a decision on the treatment of the total amount of $111,183,700 indicated in subparagraphs (a) and (b) above.

text: VII.	Summary of follow-up action taken to implement the decisions and requests of the General Assembly in its resolution 78/250 B, including the requests and recommendations of the Advisory Committee on Administrative and Budgetary Questions endorsed by the Assembly

children.text: A.	General Assembly
Financing of the United Nations Multidimensional Integrated Stabilization Mission in Mali
(Resolution 78/250 B)

table_content.text: Reaffirms the importance of cleanup and remediation activities, and requests the Secretary-General to include information on these activities in the context of the next report (para. 12).

Information on cleanup and remediation activities is provided in the sections on the closure of the Gao camp (paras. 21–25 above), the closure of Bamako headquarters (paras. 26–35 above) and the lessons learned section related to environmental mitigation and rehabilitation practices in closing peacekeeping missions (paras. 53–54 above).

children.text: B.	Advisory Committee on Administrative and Budgetary Questions
Proposed budget for the period from 1 July 2024 to 30 June 2025

table_content.text: The Advisory Committee trusts that updated information, including justification for the provision and actual expenditures for claims and adjustments for write-offs, will be provided to the General Assembly at the time of its consideration of the present report and in the final report (para. 38).

Provisions amounting to $67 million were included at the end of the budgetary period under review for liabilities such as separation costs of staff on temporary assignment to other entities, legal disputes, write-offs of uncollectible amounts, reimbursements related to damaged and abandoned contingent-owned equipment, commercial vendor claims and disputes, environmental remediation, death and disability claims and pending repatriation grant claims, among other liabilities.

The Advisory Committee notes the outposted positions and surge capacity requested for the liquidation of MINUSMA and trusts that comprehensive information on the liquidation resources both post and non-post, in all locations, including Mali, the United Nations Logistics Base, the Regional Service Centre and Headquarters, as well as information on the method used to calculate those resources, will be provided to the General Assembly at the time of its consideration of the present report and an update included in the final report (para. 52).

Resources approved for surge capacity for the period 2024/2025 amounted to $8.9 million, distributed among four entities: the Department of Management Strategy, Policy and Compliance ($1.8 million), the Department of Operational Support ($2.4 million), the United Nations Logistics Base ($3.8 million) and the Regional Service Centre ($0.9 million). These resources provided for 43 general temporary assistance (temporary replacement) positions and resources for overtime, travel, consultants, the 3R programme, information technology and other services.

The Advisory Committee notes the outposted positions and surge capacity requested for the liquidation of MINUSMA and trusts that comprehensive information on the liquidation resources both post and non-post, in all locations, including Mali, the United Nations Logistics Base, the Regional Service Centre and Headquarters, as well as information on the method used to calculate those resources, will be provided to the General Assembly at the time of its consideration of the present report and an update included in the final report (para. 52).

In addition, the liquidation entity significantly relied on standing surge capacity deployment and a number of temporary duty assignments, which were essential for providing critical expertise, specific language skills required to engage with local contractors and stakeholders and addressing staffing gaps that emerged owing to the turnover that typically occurs during the liquidation period.

children.text: Budget performance for the period from 1 July 2023 to 30 June 2024

table_content.text: The Advisory Committee trusts that information related to MINUSMA staff who were reinstated or rejoined the Organization less than 12 months after separation, as well as any related recovery of termination indemnities, will be provided to the General Assembly at the time of its consideration of the present report and in the final performance report (para. 13).
As at the date of preparation of the present report, the separation costs for 16 staff members who rejoined the Organization had been fully recovered.

The Advisory Committee trusts that information related to MINUSMA staff who were reinstated or rejoined the Organization less than 12 months after separation, as well as any related recovery of termination indemnities, will be provided to the General Assembly at the time of its consideration of the present report and in the final performance report (para. 13).
A total of 11 additional cases involving staff whose status remained withdrawn upon separation, and for whom recoveries have been made, are under review.

The Advisory Committee trusts that any lessons learned on the processing of claims related to the forced abandonment of contingent-owned equipment or hostile actions, including in relation to the calculation of reimbursable amounts, will be shared with other field missions (para. 20).

On the basis of lessons learnt from the MINUSMA drawdown/liquidation, issue papers for the 2026 COE Working Group were drafted to change the existing policy on hostile action/forced abandonment. The proposed changes include the following:

The Advisory Committee trusts that any lessons learned on the processing of claims related to the forced abandonment of contingent-owned equipment or hostile actions, including in relation to the calculation of reimbursable amounts, will be shared with other field missions (para. 20).

•	Clarification that reimbursement under hostile action/ forced abandonment applies to incidents within the designated mission area, including contingent-owned equipment under United Nations transport arrangements. All other incidents of loss and damage occurring outside the mission area (including those caused by hostile action) will be treated under the provisions of loss and damage in the transit policy.

•	Contingent-owned equipment that is beyond economical repair and long-term unserviceable will be ineligible for reimbursement under hostile action/forced abandonment and, when calculating the equipment use charge, the period of unserviceability will be disregarded.
•	Forced abandonment policy may be applied under circumstances other than safety/security, including abandonment due to extreme logistical constraints.
These policy changes would be shared with all missions if adopted by the COE Working Group in January 2026 and if approved by the General Assembly in June 2026.

The Advisory Committee notes the measures taken to ensure an environmentally compliant closure of the operation, including the participation of uniformed contingents, and trusts that lessons learned from the liquidation of MINUSMA will be duly developed and incorporated into the United Nations environmental policies and guidelines, shared with other field missions and taken into account in the planning for new missions (para. 24).

Lessons learned from the liquidation of MINUSMA are being incorporated into various United Nations environmental policies and guidelines. Building the capacity of field missions to undertake cost-efficient environmental remediation activities throughout the mission’s life cycle is a priority area of the “The Way Forward: Environment Strategy for Peace Operations 2023–2030”, under its responsibility for the cross-cutting theme. For example, to guide the participation of uniformed contingents to environmental remediation efforts, guidance on this topic has been incorporated into the United Nations Peacekeeping Missions Military Engineer Unit Manual, which now includes an annex on environmental good practices in the context of liquidation and camp closure. Similarly, the lessons learned from the liquidation of MINUSMA will be incorporated into the ongoing review of the environmental policy for peacekeeping operations and field-based special political missions and of the United Nations Environmental Management Handbook for Military Commanders in United Nations Peace Operations, including on strengthening the integration of environmental considerations into the planning of new missions and the design and building of more environmentally and resource-efficient and durable facilities.

The Advisory Committee notes that, during in situ liquidation, from August to December 2024, the Secretary-General delegated authority directly to the Director of the liquidation team and that, from December 2024 to June 2025, the Secretary-General’s authority was delegated to the Under-Secretary-General for Operational Support, who subdelegated to the Director of the liquidation team. The Committee trusts that lessons learned from the implementation of the new policy on delegation of authority during liquidation will be provided in the final performance report, including further clarification on the role of Headquarters departments and service centres in terms of shared responsibilities and on the exercise of the second line of defence (para. 27).

The delegation of authority during the closure of missions including in situ liquidation is codified in the revised Secretary-General’s bulletin on the delegation of authority in the administration of the Staff Regulations and Rules and the Financial Regulations and Rules (ST/SGB/2019/2). In terms of its implementation for MINUSMA, a representative of the Mission held authority during the in situ liquidation (e.g., Director of the MINUSMA liquidation team). Following the in situ liquidation, the Secretary-General delegated authority to the Under-Secretary-General for Operational Support for residual activities. No recommendation was made on the delegation of authority provision as a result of the MINUSMA lessons learned exercise. The enhancement of guidelines and procedures has been recommended as part of efforts to strengthen the second line of defence.

In addition, the Business Transformation and Accountability Division, pursuant to paragraph 64 of General Assembly resolution 79/257, is conducting a review of accountability of the first and second lines of defence in the areas of human resources and asset management in order to improve the culture of effective management and accountability. An update of this review will be included in the Secretary-General’s fifteenth progress report on accountability.

The Committee trusts that the lessons learned regarding the use of surge capacity for the closure of MINUSMA will help to further refine the criteria for determining the most adequate level and type of additional resources needed for future missions’ drawdown and liquidation and that information in that regard will be provided in the final performance report (para. 43).

During the liquidation period, MINUSMA ensured due diligence in planning its human resources requirements from the United Nations Logistics Base, the Regional Service Centre and Headquarters and implemented an agile staffing model to facilitate the drawdown and liquidation through a combination of modalities. These included temporary appointments and surge deployments, as required, to address evolving needs and cover capacity gaps resulting from the volatility of the drawdown and liquidation processes. Lessons learned regarding the use of surge capacity for the closure of MINUSMA are provided in paragraphs 40 to 56 of the present report.

The Committee notes that initial discussions and assessments on lessons learned from the closure of MINUSMA are taking place and trusts that updated information will be provided to the General Assembly at the time of its consideration of the present report and in the final performance report (para. 46).

Lessons learned from the closure of MINUSMA were discussed in a workshop held from 3 to 7 February 2025. Stakeholders from Headquarters and offices away from Headquarters participated in person and virtually during the workshop, at which lessons learned were discussed extensively. Stakeholders reviewed and made their contributions to the final document, which formed part of the MINUSMA end-of-mission report. This report is available to other missions through the policy and practices database.
